author: @EcycleNation
text: I have made it once about a year ago.  I didn't have a grinder, instead I cut it with knife in really fine pieces like mincing. I roasted it wet, and once it dried I called it done, after a little browning of course.  Personally I loved it.  They would have too if you hadn't told them it was from dandelion.

author: @DigGil3
text: I've read about dandilion leaf tea on a book. Coffee from its roots its novel.

author: @stevesimpson5994
text:
Oh man, I was subjected to the "delights" of dandelion tea around the age of 10.  Put it this way, it takes a brave soul to get to the bottom of the cup.

"... but it's great for your liver and kidneys" -- 95-year old father

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: Steve Simpson hehe, it's also apparently a mild laxative :)

replies.author: @deaultusername
replies.text:
+Steve Simpson 

""... but it's great for your liver and kidneys" -- 95-year old father"


old local name is pissthebed due to it being a diuretic

author: @idontwantachannelimjustcom7745
text:
Please try to even out the sound level between clips when editing a video, and also between videos.

What video software do you use?

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: I don't want a channel I'm just commenting good point, at the moment we are using openshot, bear in mind that we are on linux. What do you suggest to normalise the audio levels, audacity?

replies.author: @idontwantachannelimjustcom7745
replies.text:
this link mentions slicing the clip to change noisy parts individually.

http://www.openshotvideo.com/2009/07/audio-editing-with-openshot.html

this one mentions preprocessing the audio with audacity (free, linux)

https://answers.launchpad.net/openshot/+question/208042

and a plugin (first response has link)

http://forum.audacityteam.org/viewtopic.php?f=20&t=74531

(plugin link)
http://forum.audacityteam.org/viewtopic.php?p=167758#p167758

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: thanks

author: @richardharries5551
text: ok my GrandMother from Llanfyrnach used to make (dandelion coffee) this and she basically did what you have done but it was ALWAYS drunk with warm milk....and I'm glad that someone realises that ALL parts of a dandelion plant can be used as food/coffee/wine

author: @falcon02012
text: Garbanzo beans (chickpea) coffee: Roast chickpeas at 300° until dark brown, the color of roasted coffee beans. Then grind the beans in a coffee grinder to the same consistency you desire in regular coffee grounds. These beans seems to do better in a percolator, or boiled and then strained, rather than the quick-drip-through coffee makers.

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: falcon02012 nice one, have to try this one methinks :)

author: @johnnydoe6325
text: Did you try drying the roots *before* grinding?That's what I was taught.

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: Johnny Doe I didn't bother, it seemed to chop up quite well wet, then it dried quickly in the oven. I might try drying them in the sun first next time to see if there is any difference in taste.

replies.author: @johnnydoe6325
replies.text:
Ashley Mills  I just assumed it might work better,because you would release less oil before grinding.
Think with coffee  you don't grind the beans before roasting.you grind after.
Also you can always make Dandelion tea,which is supposed to be milder,and delicious.

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: Good point. As you say, following the coffee analogy it might make more sense to roast and then grind. Not made the tea but ive got plenty of dandelions in my patch:)  they are one of the best dynamic accumulators and airators so i leave them in the patch until they get in the way
